The Social Proof, also known as consensus, or what we might label, persuasion. People often decide what is appropriate for them to do in a situation, by examining what others are doing or have been doing there. Now, in previous modules, we've learned about the principles of reciprocity, liking, and unity, which compose the core motives, models, set of principles that work to cultivate and deepen relationships. Social Proof is the first of the next set of principles which lowers the uncertainty others feel about whether a proposed offer or idea is a good one. The principle of social proof says that, especially when people are unsure, they look to others around them, like them, to reduce that uncertainty about how they should respond. In a time like ours today, of so much information, of so many challenges, choices, and options, uncertainty has become a hallmark of modern existence. So anything, like social proof, that we can honestly provide to others that decreases the uncertainty will be something they'll want to register and use to help them to decide. And because information gathering technology continues to race ahead, social proof is going to become even more important as a way to make sense of it all.
